author: @pj_ytmt-123
text:
One of his most ambitious and promising-looking DIY. May the Lord have mercy on his soul.

I've been thinking about playing around with old desk fan motors that barely put out a breeze anymore (usually after >2 years of constant use), but after a thought experiment I decided it wasn't worth the trouble (and cost).

Here's my thought experiment: using 2 identical fans - whether as big as a standing fan or as small as a usb fan - can you use one to power up the other? No, you obviously can't unless you're constantly living with hurricanes.

It's not about losses, it's about the *source* of power: DIY projects that catch a mild breeze won't ever compare to the amount of electricity obtained from power plants (coal/gas/nuclear/hydro). Just plain fact.

Conclusion: "Size matters."

author: @direfox6095
text:
I have a limited idea of what I am talking about, but:
Newton's First Law of Productivity. First Law of Motion: An object either remains at rest or continues to move at a constant velocity, unless acted upon by an external force.

So, if you do direct load from the turbine, and it caused it to slow down and/or stop, then it requires the equivalent or perhaps more energy to get it back up to speed again, and then you have a significant augmentation in the requirements to keep the system running.
On the other half of that equation, as soon as the capacitor network becomes depleted, the devices (LED lights in this case) have a reduction in output, or cease to function altogether.
Nothing comes for free, but better management is the key when designing your needs.
A capacitor and/or battery to collect and regulate the energy input, balances a system.
There will always be some form of loss somewhere, which is transformed into some other form of energy that may not be useful for the system, but ingenuity can find ways to reduce the loss to acceptable levels, and maybe make use of the lost energy in other ways.

author: @mgabrielle2343
text:
Unfortunately If I may say your example of capacitor acting as automatic clutch does not make logical sense, but this is what is happening when you add diodes and capacitor, adding a bridge rectifier and a smoothing capacitor improves output, with the magnets arranged as N-S-N-S-N-S you are effectively producing an AC voltage, which you can check on an oscilloscope, AC voltage will light up LEDs only during a positive peaks and not during negative peaks in an AC power generator, since opposite polarity voltage cannot light LEDs during that phase, but adding a bridge rectifier helps to direct that reverse voltage into same polarity voltage, so you get stronger effect. 

Secondly when you connect all 4 coils either in parallel or in series, you may not have noticed that there could be a phase difference, so for example one coil could be producing a positive peak whilst another one may be producing negative peak or part negative peak, this will have a cancellation effect, its effect is like braking, so it can slow down the rotation or impose resistance to turning , so you may not get a strong voltage, but by connecting them together through diode rectifier you overcome that cancellation effect, and braking effect so at slow wind speeds the drum would rotate nicely and still produce good power, so by adding then capacitor you get stronger and more constant voltage, rather than a series of peaks and troughs, capacitors smooth these peaks. 
Another thing if you use Schottky diodes they have much lower forward voltage drop across them and so you get a fraction more voltage i.e. silicon diodes exabit 0.7v drop whilst Schottky diodes drop only 0.2v drop so you effectively get another half a volt advantage.

author: @BeowulfNode
text: If the wiring diagram of the options he tried is what I think they were, I think it's the full bridge rectifiers on each coil that is making it run properly. The capacitors are also hugely beneficial for providing useful DC, instead of highly fluctuating voltage. The reason I think it's the full bridge rectifiers making things work when combining the outputs of multiple coils to a single load, is that they are the preventing current being forced back through one of the other coils. Without the full bridge rectifiers, the slightly irregular spacing and placement of magnets and coils cause slightly differently timed peaks in voltage, causing the current to be driven through the other coils, and the driven coils to act as brakes. This does not matter if the coils were wired in series or parallel. Using a full bridge rectifier on each coil lets you combine the DC output sides without letting current from one coil affect any of the others, and using capacitors across the DC sides smooths the output voltage.

author: @andycorbett3052
text:
The use of the bridge rectifiers effectively isolates the coils from one another, so they are free to produce both positive and negative pulses of electricity, regardless of what is happening in the adjacent coils in terms of their voltage polarity.
You could probably leave out the rectifiers on each coil, as long as the magnet and coil alignment were matched such that any induced current was in the same polarity in each coil so that they added to one another, as against subtracted.
I'm no expert on electro-magnetics' but it may be that current passed out of phase through an adjacent coil would in effect energise that coil as an electromagnet thereby providing a breaking motion to the generator, which you noticed without the use of the rectifiers.
As an aside, the multiple 1000uF caps could probably be replaced by a single large capacitor, anywhere on the DC bus rail to achieve the same effect, which is smoothing the pulses into a more stable DC level.
Finally, love the videos and your enthusiasm for the subjects you cover. It's also refreshing to see someone just take an idea and play with it to see where it goes.
Go back long enough and the Robert Murray -Smiths of the day would have been people like Voltaire, Davy and Faraday.

author: @Chilternflyer
text: I'm late to the party and haven't read the 663 comments. Your capacitor theory is all wrong. You're forgetting first principles:- The coils are transducers. Each coil is (very likely) out of phase with it's neighbour. I.E. some coils are going to generate power and others (180 out of phase) will act as retarding motors  (powered by the generated power). The net result is that you'll potentially get no output. The bridge rectifiers are the key because they isolate the coils from each other. The capacitors also add value because they can store the peak of the output so nothing goes to waste.  Keep up the good work. This has been a great series :-)

author: @martinbroughton6655
text: Each coil is an AC generator and the number of magnets passing over the coils, along with your RPM will determine your frequency. To be able to have these all work in unison would require precision placement of the  magnets and the coils. It is of course do able but what you discovered and I love that eureka moment is converting each untimed AC wave to a common DC.  The real credit goes to the diodes in the bridge rectifier.  The capacitor depending where it was (ac or dc side) would mainly contribute to the smoothing out of any ripple. (Or unwanted back EMF if in parallel on the ac side) I couldn't quite see its position but my guess it was on the DC side. What happened in the beginning is the AC waves from one coil was creating a badly timed opposing magnetic force in another coil hence the brake effect. Well done again because although it was not what you desired you had also created dynamic braking.  Now by the time you get to about your 70th video you could use that principle to avoid over revving your generator in high winds !! Keep up this great stuff we love it.

author: @markg1051
text:
Hello Robert nice oneagain mate.
However because of my electronics background I'm having trouble understanding your results.
The capacitors have considerably larger but shorter charging currents which would introduce a short duration heavy load on the mechanics of the generator. How are you doing this, do you have the loads and rectifier/filter caps all connected to the gen and the rotor at rest and then you turn your blower on? Or do you take your load off, rev up the rotor and let everything build up in speed, charge the caps and then connect your LED panel? Curious, what is your no load DC voltage, can't recall if you mentioned that in the video? The caps once charged will draw very little top-up current, however if you have lots of capacity, they will have a huge capability to get things moving, especially if the load requires a fairly large start-up current but then settles to a much lower value when all gets operational. By the same token an empty capacitor is a like an empty battery which will draw large currents untill fully charged. In addition in many larger DC power supplies running on mains by being recified and then used to charge the cap bank in many cases will take out a fuse in the setup due to virtual short on "cold start". To get around this the designer will build in a soft start feature by limitting the charge current to a safe level and only place it into full power mode after some time at which point the caps are almost fully charged. Apologies if for any reason my questions are taken as anything other than pure curiosity. Love your content.
Cheers from down under mate and stay well.

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
Nice. 
What happens if you put them in parallel is that the output is out of phase with each other. If you connect sources in parallel that are not in phase it is effectively a short. As I told you when you short them the mechanical load on the turbine is maximum. So that's what causing it not to turn. ( Take one of your steppermotors and short all wires and turn or try to turn the shaft ... you'll understand what I'm talking about ) It is actually a good sign because it shows there is a good energy transfer from mechanical to electrical. You solved the problem by turning each ac source in a dc source which is a good solution. I guess you can call that decoupling the phase if you like.
If you got the same effect in series I don't get that at all. That would cause less current thus less mechanical load on the turbine ( and little output ). Of course if you would have outputs phase perfectly in sync you could put them parallel or in series and get good results for both situations. That would have required perfect spacing of magnets and  exact in phase placement of the transformers which is not the case.
Anyway, your solution is good. Make as many video's as you like. I think they are great.

author: @g0fvt
text: An excellent video series, fwiw you can really easily measure you rpm ACCURATELY by measuring the AC output frequency from one of the coils (many  DVMs will do this) then factor in the number of magnets. I think every pair of magnets represents a flux reversal. IE if you had perhaps 32 magnets you will get 16 cycles per revolution. FWW I think you will get more efficiency if the magnets were mounted on a common pole piece like a disc. There is a website that you might enjoy called utterpower, he has done a lot of the experiments and you might find it helpful. With random phase between your various coils it does make sense to use rectifiers, DC is easier to sum although you do drop some voltage in the diodes.

author: @davidpenson1615
text: I often considered a similar project. a few issues i see is that the further a rotating mass from its pivot point the harder it is to balance, and balance is critical when you can`t climb on your roof in a gale to stop it. a lot of commercial wind turbines are designed to deal with gale force winds with non-optimised aerofoils and resistive loading. the non-regulated coils were probably acting in semi-opposing magnetic fields effectively shunting against one another, which could be resolved observing waveforms on an oscilloscope and repositioning the coils.  the regulation idea is a good one, and efficiency could be optimised with Schottky diodes. it is better to use individual diodes as opposed to a single diode pack as power is not dissipated at a single point and are cheaper devices. i would personally use the primary transformer windings as a method of magnetic braking in high winds 8-ohm 100w resistors are very cheap. Be wary of cheap MPPT controllers 9 times out of 10 they aren`t MPPT. I would personally use a Buck converter and Arduino to maintain a constant rotational speed by increasing and decreasing electrical load.

author: @sadaavedan
text: By accident or design you have come very close to the holy grail of the free energy researchers and the technique they employ. Tesla discovered years ago that at the moment when a coil is energised or de-energised, I forget which, a great spike of energy several tens of times the energising voltage appears for around 100 milliseconds or so (and almost always dissipated away by the shunt diode/resistor in a motor). By connecting a capacitor across the coil, this spike can be accumulated and then used to perform useful work like charging a battery, or put through a bridge rectifier to light up a load etc. On battery charging this spike can be directly sent to the battery via a diode with the battery so charged demonstrating properties different to batteries charged via standard methods. This spike has also been referred to as Radiant energy and I have seen a 12V source charging an entire bank of 12V batteries and even batteries of higher voltage like 24V and 48V, albeit at a slower pace.

author: @TheEmbeddedHobbyist
text:
Without exact placement of the coils you will never have true in phase coils. And of course shorting out a coil will make it act like a magnetic brake. So by putting each coil through a rectifier you are decoupling them from each other, the capacitor only helps smooth out the voltage dips. 

To put them in parallel without rectifiers you would need to adjust the coil placement to try and get them all in phase. If the magnets are not correctly spaced apart this might be impossible. If connected with a phase difference they will see each other as part of the load and the one with the highest voltage will try and bring the lower one up to match with as much current as it can provide.

if power out demand is greater than wind power in, then it will slow down or stop. In the same way that when you cycle up a steep hill, when power out requirement is greater than you can provide power in, you slow and then get off a walk. where i live you see a lot of wind turbines just walking in the wind. :)

@ThinkingandTinkering  I thought that'd be good news.  I woke up in the middle of the night here in NY with a thought about those batteries you spoke about.  The ones from the free emergency lights you got.
You were getting small but not insignificant power from your wind generator that seemed compatible with charging each one of those batteries separately.  Is there a way to charge them all in parallel and discharge them (use them for power) in series?  You would, I think, need a way of isolated them from each other while they charge.  Perhaps transistors.  And then reconnecting them in series to use them as a hefty power source.
Just a crazy idea that woke me up.  I think you're on the right track.  And I don't think that track is too badly trampled.  You are eking out the smallest amount of power from a continuous and reliable source.  If you plot time (as is usually done) on the x or horizontal axis, and power on the y or vertical axis,  the entire area under the curve is your delivered power.  The problem is not too little breeze so much as how to capitalize on both the light breezes and the stronger wind.  Much like sailing a boat.  It requires a bit of intuition, which you have.  
Great videos.

author: @robinvince616
text: When using multiple coils in series or parallel you need to position the coils so they are all passed by the magnets at exactly the same time. Then ensure they are all connected the appropriate way round so their outputs all add together. Two coils connected in reverse parallel would fight each other and look like a short circuit which could stall the rotation. Two in series incorrectly phased would cancel giving virtually no output at all. Of course, with a separate bridge rectifier on each coil, and resevoir capacitors, the coil phasing will no longer matter, but it would be more elegant and less lossy to ensure they are all phased correctly in the first place.

author: @energycrafts8206
text:
Robert,
The capacitor effect on the rotor torque you found is one of the secrets me and a few others had discovered many years ago in doing free energy research.  Using this principe in fe devices made a big improvement in input/output efficiency.  At first I thought the improvement was due to simple cap smoothing of the rectified ac source.  Further tests proved conclusively that smoothing was not the answer and something more special was going on and not described in any literature I could find.  Point 2.  To find the ideal number of generator coils and amount of load for a given wind speed do this.  The ideal load only occurs when the impedance of a load and the generator coils are equal.  Electrically, this means when the open circuit voltage of the generator is lowered by 1/2 with a resistive load attached, then that resistance value is the equivalent ideal load impedence for max output for a given wind velocity.  Since you experienced no reduction in rotor rpm, it seems you are way under utilizing the coils and the amount of loading the rotor at 10 rpm is capable of.  ie. More coils and or heavier loading.  Great build.

author: @haukewalden2840
text:
I love these kind of projects, but I'm really wondering whether you'll ever get 1kw out of this. Well, actually, I doubt you'll ever even reach a 3-digit number. (disclaimer: I'm neither an engineer nor a physicist. I'm just curious :-)

Of course it depends heavily on the wind speed, but at 8m/s you get a theoretical maximum of 314 Watt per effective square meter. I don't know your exact measurements, but I think you said something like a radius of 80cm and a height of 60cm. That's roughly half a squaremeter, and you can use a max. half of that (probably rather a third) because in half of the area your blades are moving in the wrong direction. So, with 0.6*0.8 / 2 / 2 ~ 0.25 m², you get a theoretical max power of ~300/4 = 75 watts, but that doesn't take into account any losses caused by friction etc.

The good news is: if you have twice the windspeed, you get four times the energy. So, you'd need about 32 m/s wind in order to get >1kw with you effective surface area. However, that's 115km/h windspeed - so, you better build it very robust!

I hope I do not sound too negative, and I'd love to be proven wrong. In any case, it sure sounds like a fun project! Keep it up!

(PS: Wouldn't it help to cover the half where the blades are running against the wind? That way you should have fewer losses. In fact, maybe a kind of funnel that directs the wind to the right side could be even more helpful, I guess)

author: @alexscarbro796
text:
If you’ve accurately placed the magnets, you should be able to shuffle the coils such that they are producing waveforms that are in-phase. That way you could wire the coils in series for higher voltage or in parallel for higher current. 

My gut feeling as that series is the best option with a single high voltage rectifier.

If you really wanted to push the limits, you could arrange the coils to produce 3-phase output. 

Without any load, the coils should not stop the hub from spinning up. Only as you increase the load will it start to slow.

author: @deslomeslager
text:
As I think of it .. .. I have had 2 solar panels on my roof for years, and for a long while I thought it was easy and use them in parallel. Both got their diode, and then an mppt device. After a while I found out I was foolish. If yuo parallel up many (DC) devices only the one with the highest voltage will deliver power. In my case, one of the two panels NEVER gave much power, since the mpp was not the same as the mpp of the other panel. In your case, your load will lower the capacitor voltage (you only need one big capacitor if you want to) and several coils may add power to it. But there will be coils that can not deliver any power, because of the parallel issue. I can have a 12 volt battery of 100 Amps and a 13 volt battery of 10 Amps (connected through a diode of course, they may not send power to each other), when I draw any current, only the 13 volt battery will deliver power (until it flattens out, down to 12 Volt). Just saying .. .. In theory and practice: the power of 16 coils is not 4 times that of 4 coils, unless you really draw the maximum power out of it. At which point the rotor may slow down already .. ..
Still great to see all the effort ! I am currently working on a cheap MPPT device, since those are rather expensive and I think I can make one using cheap and existing devices already on the market (and perhaps some electronics as a ne555 chip, just a few cents a piece).

replies.author: @ZsOtherBrother
replies.text:
@Youtube Censors Opinions Nice thought, I believe you're partially correct: If the coils are out of phase, and not exactly 180°, then the rectified voltage will also be out of phase, and there would still be a voltage difference in the different coils, but smaller... which would decrease inductive resistance, or even eliminate it, (if the phase difference was originally 180°). However, since the capacitor "smooths out" the wave form, removing it may increase those temporary voltage differences, leaving you with a "partial proof".

As a side note: adding a capacitor to a coil may reduce energy losses, (if the capacitor is the right size to achieve resonance, or close to it).

author: @newtonbomb
text: I think you're on the right path here.  I think there will probably be a bit of finnicky optimization needed once you start to get more coils in though.  You could think of each coil/capacitor pair as essentially an isolated LC circuit that is going to want to run the most efficiently at a specific frequency which obviously depends on the speed of rotation of the drum vis the number of magnets; ideally you could use the multiplicity of coils to have a few differently tuned circuits resonating at frequencies representing different wind speed conditions.  You also may want to create a secondary tank capacitance which is charged by the rectified current from each individual inductor/capacitor circuit, possibly using some sort of clever transistor switching designed to maximize power output while limiting turbine braking, which would help smooth the output and further isolate the load from the turbine momentum.

replies.author: @newtonbomb
replies.text: I think the reason why it wouldn't turn while you had the coils wired in series/parallel is bc in each case the after a coil's field would collapse when the working magnets passed it they would basically dump their back emf into each other creating a field in each that would then either attract that passing magnet back to them or attract the next one and lock them into in a sort of always oppositional electromagnetic cogging effect.  The added diode bridge and capacitance is acting less like a clutch and more like digging a canal and adding a water wheel instead of creating a static lake (to give a different analogy, it would be like taking a river and creating a dam to funnel the water for the purposes of hydro-electric generation versus creating a lake with a dike for fishing or recreation only without concern for regulating or utilizing the output beyond maintaining a certain maximum water level).

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am not sure it is an LC circuit mate as the cap is after the diodes - but I really have to admit I don't know that much about electronics

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Ahh I couldn't tell from the video for sure.  It's still pretty close though, all you have to do is put it on the other side of the diode lol, youre also just a half step away from a boost/buck converter as well.  I think you should experiment a bit with the concept at least, I think you could achieve much better efficiencies than turning the coil output directly to pulsed dc.  I'm definitely no electrical engineering expert either, but I feel like resonance is the key to taking things to the next level

replies.author: @DonGeatschos
replies.text: You dont need a multi channel oscilloscope for that. Simply hook up two coils in series and measure the ac voltage using a multimeter. then adjust the position of the coils until you get maximum ac voltage. Of course this only works if the magnets on the underside are spaced evenly. Or in Sir Roberts case are not spaced at all :D

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: @ffaubert1 As Gerald stated you could roughly line up the phases by connecting the coils in series and using a multimeter, adjust one coil until you have the highest AC voltage, or if you want to be fiddly and unnecessarily precise like me, you could build a simple circuit to align the phases based on their 0 point crossing time. You would need a dual op-amp chip, two resistors, and two LEDs, and a 5V supply. You would connect the negative inputs of the op-amps and the same leg of each coil all together, then each of the other legs of the coil to one of the op-amp's positive inputs, one resistor(100-200ohms would do) to each output of the op-amp, then both LEDs between the unused ends of the resistors with one facing one way and the other reversed. Naturally the 5V supply would have its positive go to both the op-amp's VCC and it's negative would go to the op-amp's ground. Then when the magnets pass the coils, any discrepancy in phase will illuminate the LEDs, with the severity of the discrepancy reflecting in how long they stay lit, and perfect matching of phases would result in the LEDs not illuminating at all.
